medications.indication_rationale: Severe ischaemic rest pain needs opioid-level analgesia; paracetamol is inadequate. Initiated in person with falls precautions, not newly prescribed by chat.
contraindications_in_this_case: Falls risk (lives alone, exhausted) — arrange supervision/hazard removal when opioids started

medications.drug_name: antiplatelet + statin (secondary prevention) — via vascular team/GP
medications.indication_rationale: Best-medical-therapy for PAD/CLI (antiplatelet, statin, risk-factor control), started/optimised by the vascular team/GP.
Elevation and compression are for venous ulcers and are HARMFUL here — they reduce arterial perfusion and worsen ischaemia. Keep the leg dependent and warm.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Keep the affected leg dependent (hanging down) and warm (loose dry sock); keep the wound dry with a simple non-adherent dressing; do not soak, elevate or bandage tightly.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Maximises residual perfusion and protects fragile ischaemic skin while awaiting revascularisation.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Remove trip hazards and arrange support given exhaustion and new strong analgesia.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Reduces falls risk in a person living alone.

patient_education_points.point: The severe pain is your foot not getting enough blood — the arteries are badly narrowed. This needs urgent assessment by a vascular team to restore blood flow and save the limb.
Do NOT raise the leg or put a tight/compression bandage on it — that reduces the blood flow further. Keep it hanging down and warm, and keep the wound dry.
If your toes suddenly go blue or black, the foot becomes numb or you can't move it, or you develop a fever with spreading redness — call an ambulance (000).

follow_up_plan.interval: Same-day emergency/vascular assessment; then vascular surgery within 24-72h
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Perfusion, pain control, revascularisation planning, wound care, cardiovascular risk factors.
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Sudden blue/black toes, numb/paralysed foot, or fever with spreading redness — call 000 immediately.

must_include_items: Recognise an arterial ulcer / critical limb ischaemia (not venous)
Explicitly avoid leg elevation and compression; keep leg dependent and warm, wound dry
Urgent same-day in-person/vascular (or ED) assessment and escalation of analgesia
Safety-net for acute limb ischaemia and wound sepsis (000)
acceptable_alternatives: ED vs direct acute vascular service depending on availability
errors_of_commission: Advising leg elevation or compression bandaging (harmful in arterial ulcer)
Advising to walk/exercise the ischaemic limb acutely
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: LIMB-THREAT CASE: failing to recognise arterial ischaemia (and instead advising compression/elevation), or not escalating urgently, is a critical failure. The single most dangerous error is venous-ulcer management applied to an arterial ulcer.
